channel.title: osx on Jacob Sanford
channel.description: Recent content in osx on Jacob Sanford

item.title: Install PHP7 on OSX
item.description: Ensure you have OSX Sierra EOM Install PHP7 curl -s https://php-osx.liip.ch/install.sh | bash -s 7.1 Symlink Minor version into php7 dir sudo ln -s /usr/local/php5-7.1.4-20170506-100436 /usr/local/php7 Add PHP7 To Your Path Fish Shell set --universal fish_user_paths $fish_user_paths /usr/local/php7/bin Also, you should probably add the path for Bash Shell (below). Bash Shell Add /usr/local/php7/bin to /etc/paths. Set the proper timezone Edit /usr/local/php5/php.d/99-liip-developer.ini Full Instructions Here

item.title: A Solution for boot2docker issue #581?
item.description: Already discussed to death, boot2docker issue #581 (18 months ago) reported that docker volumes from the OSX disk will not allow writes if the user insider the docker container writing the file isn’t root, even with proper ownership set. We’ve looked at this before. There are several work-arounds proposed, I thought we could investigate the most promising one. The first step, however is to: Reproduce the issue On OSX, using the latest version of Docker Toolbox, provision the affected environment:

item.title: Deploying a docker-machine to a VMWare VSphere Host
item.description: Docker-Machine and VSphere By default, docker-machine on OSX leverages Virtualbox to deploy a Boot2Docker VM to serve as an endpoint for the host docker commands. This configuration satisfies a common use case for development, but does suffer from drawbacks relating to “the 3 P’s” : Performance, Persistence and Permission issues (due to VBoxSF). You can dodge some of these by provisioning Boot2Docker VM on a vSphere managed host, instead of your local computer.

item.title: Docker Toolbox, OSX, and VirtualBox VBoxSF Permissions
item.description: If you are developing in docker on OSX, chances are you’ve run into a situation described by boot2docker issue #581. The issue reports that docker volumes from the OSX disk will not allow writes if the user insider the docker container writing the file isn’t root. This has limited effect on casual development, has become an achilles heel for those wanting to use docker as the foundation of a prototype-to-production workflow AND develop on OSX.
